title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Why We Laugh: The Science of Laughter
content.text: Laughter is one of the most recognisable human sounds in the world, yet for most of history it was left to philosophers and comedians to explain. It is only in recent decades that neuroscientists, psychologists and evolutionary biologists have turned their instruments on the phenomenon. (37) [37] Lo que han descubierto es sorprendentemente complejo y desafía muchas ideas preconcebidas sobre la risa. [/37] Laughter, it turns out, is far more than a simple response to something funny. || Most laughter, research reveals, is not provoked by jokes at all. In a landmark study, psychologist Robert Provine found that the vast majority of laughter in everyday conversation follows entirely unremarkable statements — not punchlines. (38) [38] La risa funciona principalmente como una señal social que refuerza los vínculos entre personas. [/38] It signals friendliness, shared understanding and group membership, and its absence can make social interactions feel tense and cold. || The physiology of laughter is surprisingly complex. A genuine laugh — as distinct from a polite or forced one — involves the simultaneous activation of multiple brain regions, the contraction of the diaphragm and the partial suppression of normal breathing. (39) [39] Esta respuesta física intensa explica por qué reír de verdad puede dejarnos sin aliento o incluso doler. [/39] The brain is exquisitely sensitive to the difference between genuine and fake laughter, and most people can identify a forced laugh within fractions of a second. || The health benefits of laughter have attracted considerable scientific interest. Laughter triggers the release of endorphins — the same brain chemicals activated by exercise and social bonding — and temporarily reduces levels of stress hormones. (40) [40] Algunos estudios sugieren que reír con regularidad puede tener efectos positivos en el sistema inmunológico. [/40] While the evidence for 'laughter therapy' as a medical treatment remains mixed, the physiological effects of sustained laughter are measurable and broadly positive. || From an evolutionary perspective, laughter likely pre-dates language. Great apes — chimpanzees, gorillas and orangutans — all produce laughter-like vocalisations during play, suggesting a common ancestral origin. (41) [41] Esta evidencia apunta a que la risa surgió como señal de juego seguro mucho antes de que apareciera el lenguaje. [/41] In human infants, laughter emerges at around four months of age, well before the first words, reinforcing the idea that it is a deep, pre-linguistic social signal. || Humour, the cognitive trigger for laughter, is considerably more complex. It requires the ability to hold two incompatible ideas simultaneously, detect the incongruity between them and take pleasure in the resolution. (42) [42] Por eso el humor sofisticado es considerado uno de los mejores indicadores de inteligencia social y lingüística. [/42] It demands a theory of mind — the ability to imagine another person's perspective — which is why truly funny people are often socially and emotionally intelligent above all.

paragraphs.A: Cross-cultural studies show that the basic sounds of laughter are recognised and produced in virtually all human societies.
paragraphs.B: Rather than responding to humour, laughter primarily functions as a social bonding signal.
paragraphs.C: This intense physical response explains why genuine laughter can leave us breathless or even cause mild pain.
paragraphs.D: Some studies suggest that laughing regularly may have measurable positive effects on immune function.
paragraphs.E: This evidence suggests that laughter evolved as a safe-play signal long before spoken language appeared.
paragraphs.F: This is why sophisticated humour is regarded as one of the strongest indicators of social and linguistic intelligence.
paragraphs.G: What they have found is surprisingly complex and challenges many common assumptions about laughter.

questions.explanation: G introduces the complex findings of scientific research, building expectation before challenging the idea that laughter only responds to jokes.
B reframes laughter as a social signal rather than a response to humour, connecting with the explanation of its function in everyday interactions.
C explains the physical consequences of genuine laughter (breathlessness, mild pain), linking to the brain's sensitivity to fake laughter.
D adds the possible immunological benefits of laughter, before qualifying the evidence on laughter therapy.
E concludes that laughter evolved as a play signal before language, connecting with the example of human babies.
F links sophisticated humour to social and linguistic intelligence, closing the article with the idea of theory of mind.
